description:
You are a monitoring specialist who must stitch together two exported metric files so that an e-mail-alerting
    engine can ingest them.  
    Everything you need is already on disk – you only have to read, slice, rearrange and write text files
    using standard GNU/Linux command–line tools such as “cut”, “paste”, “sort”, “uniq”, “wc”, “cat”, etc.
    
    1.  Source data  
        a) /home/user/monitoring/logs/cpu_stats.csv  
           • comma-separated, **exactly 4 columns** in the order  
             timestamp,host,cpu_usage,proc_run  
           • includes a single header line followed by 4 data lines.  
        
        b) /home/user/monitoring/logs/mem_stats.csv  
           • comma-separated, **exactly 4 columns** in the order  
             timestamp,host,mem_free,swap_used  
           • also one header line + 4 data lines.  

    2.  What you have to build  
        Create a new directory if it does not yet exist:  
            /home/user/monitoring/alerts  
        and inside it create TWO new files solely with shell utilities
        (no hand-editing, no text editors):
        
        A)  /home/user/monitoring/alerts/alert_feed_20230801.txt  
            • It must contain exactly the following five lines **in this order**:

              timestamp,cpu_usage,host,swap_used
              2023-08-01T00:00:00Z,25,web01,128
              2023-08-01T00:05:00Z,45,db01,256
              2023-08-01T00:10:00Z,27,web01,130
              2023-08-01T00:15:00Z,46,db01,260

            • Construction rules the automated test will validate:
              1. Take column #1 and column #3 from cpu_stats.csv (order preserved).
              2. Take column #2 and column #4 from mem_stats.csv (order preserved).
              3. Paste the two 2-column slices **side-by-side** so the output line now has
                 four columns: timestamp,cpu_usage,host,swap_used .
              4. Keep the original first (header) line and produce four data lines,
                 total 5 lines, separated by the single character newline (0x0A) only.
              5. Separate columns with a literal comma (0x2C) – no spaces around commas,
                 no trailing separators at EOL.
                 
        B)  /home/user/monitoring/alerts/process.log  
            • Exactly four key=value pairs, one per line, **in the order shown**:

              SOURCE_CPU_LINES=5
              SOURCE_MEM_LINES=5
              OUTPUT_LINES=5
              STATUS=SUCCESS

            • SOURCE_* lines must be the line count (**including** headers)
              measured from the two original csv files.
            • OUTPUT_LINES must be the line count of alert_feed_20230801.txt .
            • STATUS must literally be SUCCESS.
            • Each line ends with a single newline (0x0A) – no extra blank lines.

    3.  Constraints  
        • Solve everything from the shell; do NOT open editors.  
        • Use only standard POSIX/GNU coreutils available in a typical distribution.  
        • Make sure the alert_feed_20230801.txt file is world-readable (mode 644) and
          process.log is owner-readable/writable only (mode 600).  
        • Create any intermediate temporary files you like but remove them before finishing.  
        • Do not modify the original csv files.

    4.  Completion criteria used by the automated grader  
        • /home/user/monitoring/alerts/alert_feed_20230801.txt exists and its SHA256 hash
          matches the reference hash of the exact five-line file shown above.  
        • /home/user/monitoring/alerts/process.log exists and contains the four exact
          lines shown above.  
        • File permissions: 644 for alert feed; 600 for process.log .  
        • No stray files left in /home/user/monitoring/alerts except the two requested ones.

    5.  Hints (not obligatory, just reminders)  
        • cut -d',' -f1,3 cpu_stats.csv → gets you the needed columns.  
        • paste -d',' slice1 slice2 → merges two equal-length files column-wise.  
        • wc -l <file> prints a pure line count useful for the log.  
        • umask/ chmod let you adjust permissions at the end.

    Deliver the above two files exactly as specified – nothing more, nothing less.
name: task_000000_f85b58b3
